title: Message Queue Abstraction Pattern for RabbitMQ in Billing
content: This DDR records the lightweight message queue abstraction pattern created while refactoring Billing away from direct RabbitMQ usage embedded in publishers and consumers. The goals are to remove RabbitMQ-specific plumbing from business-facing classes, centralize queue configuration and topology decisions, make publisher and consumer implementations small and testable, fail fast on misconfiguration, and provide a repeatable template for future domains and services.

title: Message Queue Abstraction Pattern for RabbitMQ in Billing
content: Before the refactor, Billing publishers and consumers directly owned RabbitMQ concerns such as ConnectionFactory, connection and channel lifecycle, retry loops, host and credential binding, serialization, publish calls, and queue consume setup. This mixed transport details with business logic, reduced testability, and made the pattern hard to repeat consistently.

title: Message Queue Abstraction Pattern for RabbitMQ in Billing
content: The solution split responsibilities into three layers: Core transport-neutral contracts, a dedicated RabbitMQ implementation package under LagoVista.MessageQueue.RabbitMQ, and Billing-specific adoption with thin publishers and consumers. Publishers now validate input, call IMessageQueuePublisher, and map exceptions. Consumers now implement IMessageQueueHandler<T> and call domain collaborators without managing RabbitMQ infrastructure directly.

title: Message Queue Abstraction Pattern for RabbitMQ in Billing
content: Core owns transport-neutral concepts such as IMessageQueuePublisher, IMessageQueueHandler<T>, IMessageQueueTopology, IMessageQueueTypeRegistry, MessageQueueContext<T>, MessageQueuePublishRoute, and MessageQueueSubscriptionRoute. Naming uses MessageQueue rather than broad Messaging terms because the platform already uses messaging for other concerns such as email.

title: Message Queue Abstraction Pattern for RabbitMQ in Billing
content: The RabbitMQ implementation package owns connection settings adaptation, type-to-service registration, registration validation, RabbitMQ publishing behavior, DI registration, and integration tests against real RabbitMQ in Docker. This package is the only place that should directly know about RabbitMQ.Client.

title: Message Queue Abstraction Pattern for RabbitMQ in Billing
section: Key Design Rule - CLR Types vs Topology
content: CLR types are used for message contract registration, handler binding, service ownership lookup, and duplicate detection. CLR types are not used directly as broker topology names. Topology remains explicit via DestinationName, RouteKey, and QueueName. This avoids accidental broker contract changes caused by namespace or class refactors.

title: Message Queue Abstraction Pattern for RabbitMQ in Billing
content: Misconfiguration should throw during setup rather than during first use. Examples include missing service registrations, missing message type registrations, duplicate service names, duplicate message contract registrations, message types mapped to unknown services, and missing host, username, password, or virtual host.

title: Message Queue Abstraction Pattern for RabbitMQ in Billing
content: Core should not define a universal queue connection shape. Core owns behavior contracts and route models, while the Rabbit package owns RabbitMqConnectionSettings. Existing connection objects such as IConnectionSettings are adapted into the Rabbit-specific shape during registration, including explicit virtual host mapping.

title: Message Queue Abstraction Pattern for RabbitMQ in Billing
content: Billing adopted the pattern through four application-facing classes: ImportedTransactionPublisher, PlaidEventPublisher, PlaidSyncConsumer, and TransactionIngestConsumer. These classes became significantly smaller. PlaidEventPublisher publishes a wrapper contract, PlaidSyncRequestedMessage, to carry both routing key and payload without reintroducing RabbitMQ-specific code into the publisher.

title: Message Queue Abstraction Pattern for RabbitMQ in Billing
content: A Billing-specific startup and registration class adapts Billing settings into RabbitMqConnectionSettings, explicitly sets virtual hosts, creates Billing topology, registers RabbitMQ services by logical service name, maps contract types to owning services, and registers domain publishers and handlers. The registration pattern works, though it was noted as somewhat heavy and a candidate for later cleanup.

title: Message Queue Abstraction Pattern for RabbitMQ in Billing
content: The pattern is backed by both integration and unit tests. Integration tests use Testcontainers with a real RabbitMQ broker in Docker to validate actual publish behavior. Unit tests cover RabbitMqConnectionSettings, RabbitMqServiceRegistration, RabbitMqMessageQueueTypeRegistry, RabbitMqServiceCollectionExtensions, RabbitMqTypeRegistration, RabbitMqMessageQueueBuilder, RabbitMqMessageQueuePublisher, and the Billing-facing publishers and consumers.

title: Message Queue Abstraction Pattern for RabbitMQ in Billing
content: To reuse the pattern in future domains: define a small transport-neutral contract in Core if needed, keep broker-specific behavior inside the implementation package, define domain topology explicitly, register message types to MQ services by CLR contract type, keep publishers and handlers thin, make misconfiguration throw early, add integration tests for real broker behavior, and add unit tests for registry, configuration, and domain wrappers.

title: Message Queue Abstraction Pattern for RabbitMQ in Billing
content: The refactor removed brittle RabbitMQ setup code from Billing publishers and consumers, introduced a clean transport-neutral abstraction, isolated RabbitMQ details in a dedicated package, established a repeatable registration and topology approach, added meaningful integration and unit coverage, and created a pattern expected to be reusable later in larger codebases.
